# Exodus Chapter 19 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~44r–44v (PDF pp. 84–85); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 60–61 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_019_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_019_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_019_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_019_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE SINAI THEOPHANY AND COVENANT PROPOSAL:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 19 across two pages in standard prose column format. This chapter is the hinge of the Torah — the moment toward which the entire exodus narrative has been driving and upon which everything that follows depends. YHWH descends onto Mount Sinai in fire, smoke, thunder, and earthquake to meet His people and establish a covenant with them. The chapter divides into four movements: the arrival at Sinai and temporal notice (vv. 1–2), the covenant proposal from the mountain with Yisra'el's acceptance (vv. 3–8), the announcement and preparation for the theophany (vv. 9–15), and the theophany itself with its terrifying sensory phenomena (vv. 16–25). The theological density is extraordinary: v. 4 contains the eagle-wings metaphor for divine rescue; v. 5 introduces segullah ("treasured possession"), the term for Yisra'el's unique covenantal status; v. 6 presents the dual vocation of mamlechet kohanim vegoy qadosh ("a kingdom of priests and a holy nation"); vv. 16–19 pile up every sense — sight (lightning, smoke, fire), sound (thunder, shofar, voice of God), touch (the mountain trembling) — to convey an event that ruptures all normal categories of experience. The boundary-setting (vv. 12–13, 21–24) establishes the foundational principle that the holy and the common cannot casually mix — approach to God requires preparation, mediation, and limits. The Vaticanus text (pp. 60–61) presents the LXX rendering in continuous uncial script; the chapter transition is visible on page 60, with the theophany description continuing onto page 61.

---

## Arrival at Sinai (vv. 1–2)

**1** In the third [ba-khodesh ha-shelishi] month [khodesh] of the going out [le-tset] of the sons [benei] of Yisra'el from the land [me-erets] of Mitsrayim [Egypt], on this [ha-zeh] day [ba-yom], they came [ba'u] [to the] wilderness [midbar] of Sinai.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁלִישִׁי לְצֵאת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם בַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה בָּאוּ מִדְבַּר סִינַי (ba-khodesh ha-shelishi le-tset benei-Yisra'el me-erets Mitsrayim ba-yom ha-zeh ba'u midbar Sinai, "in the third month of the going out of the sons of Yisra'el from the land of Mitsrayim, on this day, they came [to the] wilderness of Sinai"). **Key terms:** בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁלִישִׁי (ba-khodesh ha-shelishi) = "in the third month" — from חֹדֶשׁ (khodesh, "month, new moon," from חדשׁ, khadash, "to be new, to renew") + שְׁלִישִׁי (shelishi, "third," ordinal from שָׁלוֹשׁ, shalosh, "three"). The third month after the exodus (Nisan/Aviv) is Sivan (May–June), the month in which the later tradition places the festival of Shavuot (Weeks/Pentecost). The rabbis calculated that the giving of the Torah occurred on the sixth of Sivan. לְצֵאת (le-tset) = qal infinitive construct of יצא (yatsa, "to go out") — "of the going out." The exodus is not called by a noun but described by a verb in the infinitive — it is an ongoing action, a perpetual "going out." בַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה (ba-yom ha-zeh) = "on this day" — this phrase is peculiar. Which day? The text does not say "on the first day" or "on the third day" of the month. The demonstrative הַזֶּה ("this") seems to point to something immediately present, as if the narrator says "on this very day" — the day of reading, the day of hearing. The Mekhilta (de-Rabbi Yishmael) interprets: "on this day" = on the first of the month (Rosh Khodesh Sivan). But the deictic force of ha-zeh is striking — it makes the arrival at Sinai perpetually present. מִדְבַּר סִינַי (midbar Sinai) = "wilderness of Sinai" — the first occurrence of this geographic designation in the Torah. סִינַי (Sinai) — the etymology is debated: possibly from סְנֶה (seneh, "bush, thornbush"), connecting it to the burning bush of Exodus 3; or from the Mesopotamian moon-god Sin (Akkadian Su'en); or from a root meaning "clay, mud." The identification of the actual mountain is one of the great unsolved problems of biblical geography. LXX: τοῦ μηνὸς τοῦ τρίτου τῆς ἐξόδου τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ταύτῃ ἦλθοσαν εἰς τὴν ἔρημον τοῦ Σινα ("in the third month of the exodus of the sons of Israel from the land of Egypt, on this day, they came into the wilderness of Sina") — τῆς ἐξόδου ("of the exodus") renders le-tset; notably the LXX uses the noun ἔξοδος ("going out, departure") from which the English title "Exodus" derives.

**2** And they journeyed [vayis'u] from Refidim, and they came [vayavo'u] [to the] wilderness [midbar] of Sinai, and they encamped [vayakhanu] in the wilderness [ba-midbar]; and Yisra'el encamped [vayikhan] there [sham] before [neged] the mountain [ha-har].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּסְעוּ מֵרְפִידִים וַיָּבֹאוּ מִדְבַּר סִינַי וַיַּחֲנוּ בַּמִּדְבָּר וַיִּחַן־שָׁם יִשְׂרָאֵל נֶגֶד הָהָר (vayis'u me-Refidim vayavo'u midbar Sinai vayakhanu ba-midbar vayikhan-sham Yisra'el neged ha-har, "and they journeyed from Refidim, and they came [to the] wilderness of Sinai, and they encamped in the wilderness; and Yisra'el encamped there before the mountain"). **Key terms:** וַיִּסְעוּ (vayis'u) = qal of נסע (nasa, "to pull up [tent stakes], journey, set out, depart") — "and they journeyed." The verb nasa captures the physical act of breaking camp: pulling up stakes and moving on. מֵרְפִידִים (me-Refidim) = "from Refidim" — the location of the water-from-rock episode (17:1–7) and the battle with Amaleq (17:8–16). The name may derive from רפד (rafad, "to spread out, support"). **GRAMMATICALLY CRITICAL — the shift from plural to singular:** The verse begins with three plural verbs: וַיִּסְעוּ (vayis'u, "they journeyed"), וַיָּבֹאוּ (vayavo'u, "they came"), וַיַּחֲנוּ (vayakhanu, "they encamped") — treating Yisra'el as a collection of individuals. Then the fourth verb shifts to singular: וַיִּחַן (vayikhan, "he/it encamped") — treating Yisra'el as a single entity. Rashi (following Mekhilta) famously notes: "as one man with one heart" (ke-ish ekhad be-lev ekhad) — at Sinai, Yisra'el achieved a unity of purpose unmatched anywhere else in their journey. Every other encampment is described with plural verbs (indicating dissension and division); only here does the singular appear. נֶגֶד (neged) = "before, in front of, opposite, facing" — Yisra'el encamped facing the mountain. The mountain is in their field of vision — they see the place where God will descend. הָהָר (ha-har) = "the mountain" — the definite article indicates the mountain par excellence, the well-known mountain, Mount Sinai/Khorev. LXX: καὶ ἐξῆραν ἐκ Ραφιδιν καὶ ἤλθοσαν εἰς τὴν ἔρημον τοῦ Σινα καὶ παρενέβαλεν ἐκεῖ Ισραηλ κατέναντι τοῦ ὄρους ("and they departed from Raphidin and came into the wilderness of Sina, and Israel encamped there opposite the mountain") — the LXX collapses the four verbs into three, losing the plural-to-singular shift. κατέναντι ("opposite, in front of") for neged.

---

## The Covenant Proposal (vv. 3–8)

**3** And Mosheh went up [alah] to ha-Elohim [God], and YHWH called [vayiqra] to him from the mountain [min-ha-har], saying [le'mor]: "Thus [koh] you shall say [tomar] to the house [le-veit] of Ya'aqov [Jacob], and you shall declare [ve-taggid] to the sons [li-vnei] of Yisra'el:

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּמֹשֶׁה עָלָה אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים וַיִּקְרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה מִן־הָהָר לֵאמֹר כֹּה תֹאמַר לְבֵית יַעֲקֹב וְתַגֵּיד לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (u-Mosheh alah el-ha-Elohim vayiqra elav YHWH min-ha-har le'mor koh tomar le-veit Ya'aqov ve-taggid li-vnei Yisra'el, "and Mosheh went up to God, and YHWH called to him from the mountain, saying: 'Thus you shall say to the house of Ya'aqov, and you shall declare to the sons of Yisra'el'"). **Key terms:** עָלָה (alah) = qal perfect of עלה (alah, "to go up, ascend") — "went up." The fundamental verb of vertical movement toward God. Mosheh ascends; God descends. The mountain is the meeting point between heaven and earth. וַיִּקְרָא (vayiqra) = qal of קרא (qara, "to call, cry out, summon, proclaim") — "and he called." YHWH initiates the communication. God calls; Mosheh listens. The covenant begins with divine initiative, not human aspiration. **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — "house of Ya'aqov" // "sons of Yisra'el":** The parallelism between לְבֵית יַעֲקֹב (le-veit Ya'aqov, "to the house of Ya'aqov") and לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (li-vnei Yisra'el, "to the sons of Yisra'el") uses the patriarch's two names — Ya'aqov (the birth name, "heel-grasper," the name of struggle and vulnerability) and Yisra'el (the God-given name, "one who strives with God," the name of triumph). The Mekhilta and later rabbinic tradition interpret: "house of Ya'aqov" = the women (say it gently, with the tender language of a household); "sons of Yisra'el" = the men (declare it with the forceful language of covenant obligation). Whether or not one accepts this gendered distinction, the dual address encompasses the entire community. תֹאמַר (tomar) = "you shall say" — the gentle verb (אמר, amar, "to say"). וְתַגֵּיד (ve-taggid) = hiphil of נגד (nagad, "to tell, declare, announce, make known") — "you shall declare." The hiphil is more forceful than amar — it means to lay out clearly, to confront with information. LXX: καὶ Μωυσῆς ἀνέβη εἰς τὸ ὄρος τοῦ θεοῦ· καὶ ἐκάλεσεν αὐτὸν ὁ θεὸς ἐκ τοῦ ὄρους λέγων· τάδε ἐρεῖς τῷ οἴκῳ Ιακωβ καὶ ἀναγγελεῖς τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ ("and Mosheh went up to the mountain of God; and God called him from the mountain, saying: 'These things you shall say to the house of Iakob and announce to the sons of Israel'") — the LXX reads "God" (ὁ θεός) rather than "YHWH" for the caller.

**4** 'You yourselves [attem] have seen [re'item] what [asher] I did [asiti] to Mitsrayim [Egypt], and I carried [va-essa] you upon [al] wings [kanfei] of nesharim [eagles/vultures], and I brought [va-avi] you to me [elai].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אַתֶּם רְאִיתֶם אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתִי לְמִצְרָיִם וָאֶשָּׂא אֶתְכֶם עַל־כַּנְפֵי נְשָׁרִים וָאָבִא אֶתְכֶם אֵלָי (attem re'item asher asiti le-Mitsrayim va-essa etkhem al-kanfei nesharim va-avi etkhem elai, "you yourselves have seen what I did to Mitsrayim, and I carried you upon wings of eagles, and I brought you to me"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the eagle-wings metaphor:** אַתֶּם רְאִיתֶם (attem re'item) = "you yourselves have seen" — the emphatic independent pronoun אַתֶּם (attem, "you," 2mp) + qal perfect 2mp of ראה (ra'ah, "to see"). This is not hearsay; this is eyewitness testimony. God appeals to direct experience. What follows is not mythology but history as the audience experienced it. וָאֶשָּׂא (va-essa) = qal of נשׂא (nasa, "to lift, carry, bear") with the vav-consecutive — "and I carried/lifted." The same verb used for carrying a burden, lifting a child, bearing a load. God bore Yisra'el. כַּנְפֵי נְשָׁרִים (kanfei nesharim) = "wings of eagles/vultures" — from כָּנָף (kanaf, "wing, edge, extremity") + נֶשֶׁר (nesher, "eagle" or "griffon vulture"). The נֶשֶׁר is the largest soaring bird in the ancient Near East — likely the griffon vulture (Gyps fulvus), with a wingspan of up to 2.8 meters, though "eagle" is the traditional rendering. Deut 32:11 expands the metaphor: "As a nesher stirs up its nest, hovers over its young, spreads out its wings, takes them, bears them on its pinions — YHWH alone guided him." The image is of a parent bird carrying its young on its wings — an act of protection, speed, and effortless transport. Rashi explains: other birds carry their young in their talons (fearing birds of prey above); the nesher carries its young on its back (fearing only the hunter's arrow below, it says: "better the arrow strike me than my child"). וָאָבִא (va-avi) = hiphil of בוא (bo, "to come, bring") — "and I brought." The destination is אֵלָי (elai) = "to me" — the entire exodus was not merely rescue from Egypt but movement toward God. The destination of redemption is relationship. LXX: αὐτοὶ ἑωράκατε ὅσα πεποίηκα τοῖς Αἰγυπτίοις καὶ ἀνέλαβον ὑμᾶς ὡσεὶ ἐπὶ πτερύγων ἀετῶν καὶ προσηγαγόμην ὑμᾶς πρὸς ἐμαυτόν ("you yourselves have seen what things I did to the Egyptians, and I took you up as upon wings of eagles, and I brought you near to myself") — ὡσεί ("as, as if") is added, making the metaphor explicitly a simile; πτέρυξ ἀετῶν ("wings of eagles") for kanfei nesharim; προσάγω ("bring near, lead to") for va-avi.

**5** And now [ve-attah], if [im] you will truly hear [shamo'a tishme'u] my voice [be-qoli], and you will keep [u-shmartem] my covenant [et-beriti], then you will be [vi-heyitem] for me [li] a segullah [treasured possession] from all [mi-kol] the peoples [ha-ammim], for [ki] to me [li] [belongs] all [kol] the earth [ha-arets].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעַתָּה אִם־שָׁמוֹעַ תִּשְׁמְעוּ בְּקֹלִי וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת־בְּרִיתִי וִהְיִיתֶם לִי סְגֻלָּה מִכָּל־הָעַמִּים כִּי־לִי כָּל־הָאָרֶץ (ve-attah im-shamo'a tishme'u be-qoli u-shmartem et-beriti vi-heyitem li segullah mi-kol-ha-ammim ki-li kol-ha-arets, "and now, if hearing you will hear my voice and you will keep my covenant, then you will be for me a treasured possession from all the peoples, for to me [belongs] all the earth"). **Key terms:** שָׁמוֹעַ תִּשְׁמְעוּ (shamo'a tishme'u) = infinitive absolute + imperfect of שׁמע (shama, "to hear, listen, obey") — "hearing you will hear" = "you will truly/surely hear/obey." The infinitive absolute construction intensifies the verb: this is not casual listening but deep, obedient hearing. שׁמע in Hebrew encompasses hearing, understanding, and obeying — the three are inseparable. בְּקֹלִי (be-qoli) = "in/to my voice" — from קוֹל (qol, "voice, sound") + 1cs suffix. The preposition בְּ (be, "in") with שׁמע means "to listen to, to obey." The voice of YHWH is the voice they will hear at Sinai (v. 19). וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם (u-shmartem) = qal perfect 2mp of שׁמר (shamar, "to keep, guard, watch, observe, preserve") — "and you will keep." Shamar means active, vigilant guarding — the way a watchman guards a city, the way a shepherd guards a flock. בְּרִיתִי (beriti) = "my covenant" — from בְּרִית (berit, "covenant, pact, treaty, alliance") + 1cs suffix. The root is debated: possibly from ברה (barah, "to eat," since covenant-making involved a shared meal) or from ברא in Akkadian (baru, "to bind, fetter"). A berit is a binding relationship established by solemn promise and ritual. **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — SEGULLAH:** סְגֻלָּה (segullah) = "treasured possession, special property, personal treasure." This is one of the most important theological terms in the Hebrew Bible. The word appears only 8 times in the OT (Ex 19:5; Deut 7:6; 14:2; 26:18; 1 Chr 29:3; Ps 135:4; Eccl 2:8; Mal 3:17). In Akkadian, sikiltu/sikultu refers to a king's personal treasure or private property — wealth kept apart from the general treasury for the king's personal use. Yisra'el is YHWH's private treasure among the nations — not merely favored, but personally possessed. The conditional אִם (im, "if") is crucial: segullah status is covenantal, not automatic. It depends on shamo'a tishme'u ("truly hearing/obeying"). כִּי־לִי כָּל־הָאָרֶץ (ki-li kol-ha-arets) = "for to me [belongs] all the earth" — this clause prevents any misunderstanding: Yisra'el's special status does not mean the other nations do not belong to God. All the earth is God's. Yisra'el is chosen from among nations that all belong to YHWH — making the election more remarkable, not less. LXX: καὶ νῦν ἐὰν ἀκοῇ ἀκούσητε τῆς ἐμῆς φωνῆς καὶ φυλάξητε τὴν διαθήκην μου, ἔσεσθέ μοι λαὸς περιούσιος ἀπὸ πάντων τῶν ἐθνῶν· ἐμὴ γάρ ἐστιν πᾶσα ἡ γῆ ("and now, if by hearing you hear my voice and keep my covenant, you shall be for me a special/chosen people from all the nations; for all the earth is mine") — **LXX KEY RENDERING:** λαὸς περιούσιος ("a people beyond [others], a special people") for segullah — the LXX interprets segullah as a quality of the people (periousios, "abundant, special, beyond") rather than as a noun ("treasured possession"). This rendering influenced the NT (Titus 2:14, λαὸν περιούσιον). διαθήκη ("testament, covenant, arrangement") for berit — the LXX consistently renders berit as diatheke, which in Greek emphasizes a unilateral disposition (testament/will) rather than a bilateral agreement (syntheke), reflecting the theological understanding that God's covenant is primarily a gift, not a negotiation.

**6** And you [ve-attem] shall be [tihyu] for me a kingdom [mamlechet] of priests [kohanim] and a nation [vegoy] holy [qadosh].' These [elleh] [are] the words [ha-devarim] that you shall speak [tedabber] to the sons [el-benei] of Yisra'el."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאַתֶּם תִּהְיוּ־לִי מַמְלֶכֶת כֹּהֲנִים וְגוֹי קָדוֹשׁ אֵלֶּה הַדְּבָרִים אֲשֶׁר תְּדַבֵּר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (ve-attem tihyu-li mamlechet kohanim vegoy qadosh elleh ha-devarim asher tedabber el-benei Yisra'el, "and you shall be for me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation. These [are] the words that you shall speak to the sons of Yisra'el"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — MAMLECHET KOHANIM VEGOY QADOSH:** This is one of the most consequential phrases in all of Scripture. מַמְלֶכֶת (mamlechet) = "kingdom of" — from the root מלך (malakh, "to reign, be king"). The construct form mamlechet indicates "a kingdom consisting of" or "a kingdom characterized by." כֹּהֲנִים (kohanim) = "priests" — plural of כֹּהֵן (kohen, "priest, minister, one who serves before God"). The kohen stands between God and people, mediating the divine to the human and the human to the divine. A "kingdom of priests" means the entire nation functions as a priesthood — not that every individual is a priest in the Aaronic/Levitical sense, but that the nation corporately mediates God's presence to the world as a priest mediates God's presence to the community. Yisra'el is to the nations what the priesthood is to Yisra'el. גוֹי (goy) = "nation" — from a root meaning "body, people group." This word, later specialized to mean "non-Israelite nation" (gentile), here means simply "nation, people." קָדוֹשׁ (qadosh) = "holy, set apart, separate, consecrated" — from קדשׁ (qadash, "to be holy, to be set apart, to consecrate"). Qadosh is the fundamental attribute of God in the Hebrew Bible — to be holy is to be utterly other, separate from the profane, burning with divine purity. A "holy nation" is a nation set apart from common use, consecrated for God's purposes, distinguished from all other nations by its relationship to the Holy One. The double designation — mamlechet kohanim AND goy qadosh — establishes Yisra'el's dual vocation: priestly service (mediating God to the world) and holy separation (being set apart for God). These are not alternatives but complementary dimensions of the same calling. This verse is quoted in 1 Peter 2:9: "You are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession" — applying Yisra'el's Sinai vocation to the community of Messiah-followers. LXX: ὑμεῖς δὲ ἔσεσθέ μοι βασίλειον ἱεράτευμα καὶ ἔθνος ἅγιον· ταῦτα τὰ ῥήματα ἐρεῖς τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ ("and you shall be for me a royal priesthood and a holy nation; these words you shall speak to the sons of Israel") — **LXX KEY RENDERING:** βασίλειον ἱεράτευμα ("royal priesthood") for mamlechet kohanim — the LXX reads the construct chain as an adjective + noun ("royal priesthood") rather than a noun + noun ("kingdom of priests"). This shifts the emphasis from a political entity (kingdom) composed of priests to a priestly order characterized as royal. The NT (1 Pet 2:9) follows the LXX reading: βασίλειον ἱεράτευμα. ἔθνος ἅγιον ("holy nation") for goy qadosh.

**7** And Mosheh came [vayavo] and he called [vayiqra] for the elders [le-ziqnei] of the people [ha-am], and he set [vayasem] before them [lifneihem] all [et-kol] these [ha-elleh] words [ha-devarim] that YHWH commanded him [tsivvahu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּבֹא מֹשֶׁה וַיִּקְרָא לְזִקְנֵי הָעָם וַיָּשֶׂם לִפְנֵיהֶם אֵת כָּל־הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה אֲשֶׁר צִוָּהוּ יְהוָה (vayavo Mosheh vayiqra le-ziqnei ha-am vayasem lifneihem et kol-ha-devarim ha-elleh asher tsivvahu YHWH, "and Mosheh came and he called for the elders of the people, and he set before them all these words that YHWH commanded him"). **Key terms:** וַיָּשֶׂם (vayasem) = qal of שׂים/שׂוּם (sim/sum, "to put, place, set") — "and he set/placed." Mosheh "places" the words before them — the image is of laying something tangible on a table. Words are treated as objects to be examined, weighed, considered. לִפְנֵיהֶם (lifneihem) = "before them, before their faces" — from פָּנִים (panim, "face") + 3mp suffix. The words are placed face to face with the elders. זִקְנֵי (ziqnei) = "elders of" — construct plural of זָקֵן (zaqen, "elder, old man," from זָקָן, zaqan, "beard"). The elders are the representative heads of the tribal-clan structure, the leadership layer between Mosheh and the common people. צִוָּהוּ (tsivvahu) = piel of צוה (tsavah, "to command, charge, commission") + 3ms suffix — "that he commanded him." Mosheh is under divine command to deliver these exact words. LXX: ἦλθεν δὲ Μωυσῆς καὶ ἐκάλεσεν τοὺς πρεσβυτέρους τοῦ λαοῦ καὶ παρέθηκεν αὐτοῖς πάντας τοὺς λόγους τούτους, οὓς συνέταξεν αὐτῷ ὁ θεός ("and Mosheh came and called the elders of the people, and set before them all these words which God commanded him") — παρατίθημι ("set before, place beside") for vayasem; συντάσσω ("command together, arrange") for tsivvahu.

**8** And all [kol] the people [ha-am] answered [vaya'anu] together [yakhdav] and they said [vayomeru]: "All [kol] that YHWH has spoken [dibber] we will do [na'aseh]." And Mosheh brought back [vayashev] the words [et-divrei] of the people [ha-am] to YHWH.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּעֲנוּ כָל־הָעָם יַחְדָּו וַיֹּאמְרוּ כֹּל אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה נַעֲשֶׂה וַיָּשֶׁב מֹשֶׁה אֶת־דִּבְרֵי הָעָם אֶל־יְהוָה (vaya'anu khol-ha-am yakhdav vayomeru kol asher dibber YHWH na'aseh vayashev Mosheh et-divrei ha-am el-YHWH, "and all the people answered together and they said: 'All that YHWH has spoken we will do.' And Mosheh brought back the words of the people to YHWH"). **Key terms:** יַחְדָּו (yakhdav) = "together, unitedly, as one" — an adverb from the root יחד (yakhad, "to be united, to be one"). Like the singular verb vayikhan in v. 2, this emphasizes the unanimity of the response. The entire people, as one, accepts the covenant. נַעֲשֶׂה (na'aseh) = qal imperfect 1cp of עשׂה (asah, "to do, make, act") — "we will do." This is the first occurrence of the na'aseh formula that becomes decisive at 24:3, 7 (where the people will say na'aseh ve-nishma, "we will do and we will hear/obey" — commitment before comprehension). Here at 19:8 the response is simply na'aseh — unconditional acceptance of YHWH's covenant terms before the specific commandments have been given. The people accept the relationship before knowing the details of its obligations. וַיָּשֶׁב (vayashev) = hiphil of שׁוב (shuv, "to return, turn back, bring back") — "and he brought back, he returned." Mosheh serves as shuttle diplomat between God on the mountain and the people below. He carries God's proposal down and the people's acceptance up. The verb shuv in hiphil means to cause to return — Mosheh causes the words to return to their origin (YHWH). LXX: καὶ ἀπεκρίθη πᾶς ὁ λαὸς ὁμοθυμαδὸν καὶ εἶπαν· πάντα ὅσα εἶπεν ὁ θεός, ποιήσομεν καὶ ἀκουσόμεθα· ἀνήνεγκεν δὲ Μωυσῆς τοὺς λόγους τοῦ λαοῦ πρὸς τὸν θεόν ("and all the people answered with one mind and said: 'All that God has said, we will do and we will hear'; and Mosheh brought up the words of the people to God") — **LXX CRITICAL ADDITION:** The LXX adds καὶ ἀκουσόμεθα ("and we will hear/obey") — this anticipates the fuller formula of 24:7 (na'aseh ve-nishma). The MT here has only na'aseh. ὁμοθυμαδόν ("with one mind, unanimously") for yakhdav — a stronger rendering than "together."

## Preparation for the Theophany (vv. 9–15)

**9** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Behold [hinneh], I [anokhi] [am] coming [ba] to you in a thickness [be-av] of the cloud [he-anan], in order that [ba-avur] the people will hear [yishma] when I speak [be-dabberi] with you, and also [ve-gam] in you [bekha] they will trust [ya'aminu] forever [le-olam]." And Mosheh declared [vayagged] the words [et-divrei] of the people [ha-am] to YHWH.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי בָּא אֵלֶיךָ בְּעַב הֶעָנָן בַּעֲבוּר יִשְׁמַע הָעָם בְּדַבְּרִי עִמָּךְ וְגַם־בְּךָ יַאֲמִינוּ לְעוֹלָם וַיַּגֵּד מֹשֶׁה אֶת־דִּבְרֵי הָעָם אֶל־יְהוָה (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh hinneh anokhi ba elekha be-av he-anan ba-avur yishma ha-am be-dabberi immakh ve-gam-bekha ya'aminu le-olam vayagged Mosheh et-divrei ha-am el-YHWH, "and YHWH said to Mosheh: 'Behold, I [am] coming to you in a thickness of the cloud, in order that the people will hear when I speak with you, and also in you they will trust forever.' And Mosheh declared the words of the people to YHWH"). **Key terms:** בְּעַב (be-av) = "in a thickness, in a density" — from עָב (av, "thickness, dark cloud, cloud-mass"). The word is distinct from עָנָן (anan, "cloud") — av is the dense, dark core of the cloud, the impenetrable mass. הֶעָנָן (he-anan) = "the cloud" — from עָנָן (anan, "cloud"). The cloud (anan) is the visible form of divine presence — it guided them as a pillar (13:21–22), it covered the mountain (24:15–16), it will fill the mishkan (40:34–35). Here God comes in the av he-anan, the "thick part of the cloud" — not a wispy haze but a dense, overwhelming mass of divine presence that both reveals and conceals. בַּעֲבוּר (ba-avur) = "in order that, for the sake of, so that" — a purpose clause. God makes His voice audible to the people for a specific purpose: to validate Mosheh's prophetic authority. יַאֲמִינוּ (ya'aminu) = hiphil of אמן (aman, "to be firm, to be faithful, to trust, to believe") — "they will trust, they will believe." The hiphil of אמן means "to regard as reliable, to trust in." This is the root from which אָמֵן (amen, "truly, so be it, it is firm") derives. Trust in Mosheh is grounded in the people's direct experience of hearing God speak to him. לְעוֹלָם (le-olam) = "forever, for the age" — from עוֹלָם (olam, "long duration, eternity, age, world-age"). The trust is not temporary but permanent. The last clause — "and Mosheh declared the words of the people to YHWH" — seems redundant with 19:8b. Some scholars see this as evidence of a doublet or redactional seam; others suggest Mosheh's second report includes additional details of the people's response. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ παραγίνομαι πρὸς σὲ ἐν στύλῳ νεφέλης, ἵνα ἀκούσῃ ὁ λαὸς λαλοῦντός μου πρὸς σὲ καὶ σοὶ πιστεύσωσιν εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα ("and the Lord said to Mosheh: 'Behold, I am coming to you in a pillar of cloud, that the people may hear me speaking to you and may believe you forever'") — **LXX VARIANT:** ἐν στύλῳ νεφέλης ("in a pillar of cloud") for be-av he-anan — the LXX harmonizes with the pillar-of-cloud imagery from 13:21, whereas the MT uses the distinct term av ("thickness/dark-cloud-mass").

**10** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Go [lekh] to the people and you shall sanctify them [ve-qiddashtam] today [ha-yom] and tomorrow [u-makhar], and they shall wash [ve-kibbsu] their garments [simlatam].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵךְ אֶל־הָעָם וְקִדַּשְׁתָּם הַיּוֹם וּמָחָר וְכִבְּסוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh lekh el-ha-am ve-qiddashtam ha-yom u-makhar ve-kibbsu simlatam, "and YHWH said to Mosheh: 'Go to the people and sanctify them today and tomorrow, and they shall wash their garments'"). **Key terms:** וְקִדַּשְׁתָּם (ve-qiddashtam) = piel perfect 2ms of קדשׁ (qadash, "to be holy, to set apart, to consecrate") + 3mp suffix — "and you shall sanctify/consecrate them." The piel (intensive/causative) of qadash means to make something holy, to set it apart from ordinary use, to prepare it for contact with the divine. Mosheh is to consecrate the entire people — a ritual process involving purification and behavioral restrictions (v. 15). הַיּוֹם וּמָחָר (ha-yom u-makhar) = "today and tomorrow" — a two-day preparation period before the theophany on the third day (v. 11). וְכִבְּסוּ (ve-kibbsu) = piel of כבס (kavas, "to wash, launder [garments]") — "and they shall wash." The verb kavas specifically means washing cloth/garments (as opposed to רחץ, rakhats, which is washing the body). The piel intensive suggests thorough, vigorous washing. שִׂמְלֹתָם (simlatam) = "their garments" — from שִׂמְלָה (simlah, "mantle, wrapper, outer garment, cloak") + 3mp suffix. The washing of garments is a physical act with spiritual significance: external cleanliness symbolizes internal purification. One does not approach the Holy One in soiled clothing. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· καταβὰς διαμάρτυραι τῷ λαῷ καὶ ἅγνισον αὐτοὺς σήμερον καὶ αὔριον, καὶ πλυνάτωσαν τὰ ἱμάτια ("and the Lord said to Mosheh: 'Go down and solemnly charge the people, and purify them today and tomorrow, and let them wash their garments'") — **LXX ADDITION:** διαμαρτύρομαι ("solemnly charge, earnestly warn") is added; ἁγνίζω ("purify, consecrate") for ve-qiddashtam; πλύνω ("wash") for ve-kibbsu.

**11** And they shall be [ve-hayu] prepared [nekhonim] for the third [la-yom ha-shelishi] day [yom], for on the third [ba-yom ha-shelishi] day YHWH will come down [yered] before the eyes of [le-einei] all the people [kol-ha-am] upon [al] Mount [har] Sinai.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיוּ נְכֹנִים לַיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי כִּי בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי יֵרֵד יְהוָה לְעֵינֵי כָל־הָעָם עַל־הַר סִינָי (ve-hayu nekhonim la-yom ha-shelishi ki ba-yom ha-shelishi yered YHWH le-einei khol-ha-am al-har Sinai, "and they shall be prepared for the third day, for on the third day YHWH will come down before the eyes of all the people upon Mount Sinai"). **Key terms:** נְכֹנִים (nekhonim) = niphal participle plural of כון (kun, "to be firm, established, prepared, ready") — "prepared, ready, established." The niphal of kun means to be in a state of readiness — spiritually, ritually, and mentally prepared for what is about to happen. יֵרֵד (yered) = qal imperfect of ירד (yarad, "to go down, descend, come down") — "he will come down, he will descend." **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — divine descent:** This is one of the most extraordinary claims in the Hebrew Bible. YHWH will physically descend (yarad) onto a specific mountain before the eyes of an entire nation. The verb yarad is the inverse of alah (v. 3) — Mosheh goes up, God comes down. The condescension of the Creator to descend to a point in space and time, visible to human eyes, is the core scandal of biblical theism. לְעֵינֵי (le-einei) = "to/before the eyes of" — from עַיִן (ayin, "eye") in construct plural + preposition. The descent will be visible — not hidden, not private, not mystical, but public and sensory. כָל־הָעָם (khol-ha-am) = "all the people" — every member of the community will witness the theophany. LXX: καὶ ἔστωσαν ἕτοιμοι εἰς τὴν ἡμέραν τὴν τρίτην· τῇ γὰρ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ καταβήσεται κύριος ἐπὶ τὸ ὄρος τὸ Σινα ἐναντίον παντὸς τοῦ λαοῦ ("and let them be ready for the third day; for on the third day the Lord will come down upon Mount Sina before all the people") — ἕτοιμος ("ready, prepared") for nekhonim; καταβαίνω ("come down, descend") for yered.

**12** And you shall set bounds [ve-higbalta] [for] the people round about [saviv], saying [le'mor]: 'Guard yourselves [hishameru lakhem] from going up [alot] on the mountain [ba-har] or touching [u-negoa] its edge [be-qatsehu]; anyone [kol] touching [ha-nogea] the mountain [ba-har] shall surely be put to death [mot yumat].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהִגְבַּלְתָּ אֶת־הָעָם סָבִיב לֵאמֹר הִשָּׁמְרוּ לָכֶם עֲלוֹת בָּהָר וּנְגֹעַ בְּקָצֵהוּ כָּל־הַנֹּגֵעַ בָּהָר מוֹת יוּמָת (ve-higbalta et-ha-am saviv le'mor hishameru lakhem alot ba-har u-negoa be-qatsehu kol-ha-nogea ba-har mot yumat, "and you shall set bounds [for] the people round about, saying: 'Guard yourselves from going up on the mountain or touching its edge; anyone touching the mountain shall surely be put to death'"). **Key terms:** וְהִגְבַּלְתָּ (ve-higbalta) = hiphil perfect 2ms of גבל (gaval, "to set a boundary, mark a border") — "and you shall set bounds/border." From the root גְּבוּל (gevul, "boundary, border, territory"). Mosheh must physically mark off a perimeter around the mountain that the people must not cross. The holy space requires boundaries — the concept of qedushah (holiness) is inherently spatial as well as moral. סָבִיב (saviv) = "round about, surrounding, on every side." The boundary encircles the entire mountain. הִשָּׁמְרוּ לָכֶם (hishameru lakhem) = niphal imperative 2mp of שׁמר (shamar, "to guard") + reflexive dative — "guard yourselves" — the reflexive: you are the ones who must keep yourselves from violation. עֲלוֹת (alot) = qal infinitive construct of עלה (alah, "to go up") — "going up, ascending." The same verb used of Mosheh's ascent in v. 3 — but what is permitted to Mosheh alone is forbidden to the people. וּנְגֹעַ (u-negoa) = qal infinitive construct of נגע (naga, "to touch, reach, strike") — "or touching." Even touching the edge (qatseh) of the mountain is lethal. מוֹת יוּמָת (mot yumat) = infinitive absolute + hophal imperfect of מות (mut, "to die") — "dying he shall be put to death" = "he shall surely/certainly be put to death." The death penalty formula — the most severe sanction in biblical law. LXX: καὶ ὁριεῖς τὸν λαὸν κύκλῳ λέγων· προσέχετε ἑαυτοῖς τοῦ ἀναβῆναι εἰς τὸ ὄρος καὶ θιγεῖν τι αὐτοῦ· πᾶς ὁ ἁψάμενος τοῦ ὄρους θανάτῳ τελευτήσει ("and you shall set bounds for the people round about, saying: 'Take heed to yourselves not to go up to the mountain or to touch any part of it; everyone touching the mountain shall surely die'") — ὁρίζω ("set bounds, define") for ve-higbalta; θάνατος + τελευτάω ("by death shall he come to an end") for mot yumat.

**13** No hand [yad] shall touch [tigga] him [bo]; for he shall surely be stoned [ki-saqol yissaqel] or surely shot through [o-yaroh yiyareh]; whether [im] beast [behemah] or [im] man [ish], he shall not live [lo yikhyeh]. When the yovel [ram's horn] sounds a long blast [bimshokh], they [hemmah] shall go up [ya'alu] on the mountain [ba-har]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא־תִגַּע בּוֹ יָד כִּי־סָקוֹל יִסָּקֵל אוֹ־יָרֹה יִיָּרֶה אִם־בְּהֵמָה אִם־אִישׁ לֹא יִחְיֶה בִּמְשֹׁךְ הַיֹּבֵל הֵמָּה יַעֲלוּ בָהָר (lo-tigga bo yad ki-saqol yissaqel o-yaroh yiyareh im-behemah im-ish lo yikhyeh bimshokh ha-yovel hemmah ya'alu va-har, "no hand shall touch him; for he shall surely be stoned or surely be shot through; whether beast or man, he shall not live. When the yovel sounds a long blast, they shall go up on the mountain"). **Key terms:** לֹא־תִגַּע בּוֹ יָד (lo-tigga bo yad) = "no hand shall touch him" — the violator of the boundary must not even be touched to be executed (lest the executioner also become contaminated by contact). He must be killed from a distance. סָקוֹל יִסָּקֵל (saqol yissaqel) = infinitive absolute + niphal imperfect of סקל (saqal, "to stone, pelt with stones") — "he shall surely be stoned." Stoning is execution from a distance — no hand touches the condemned. יָרֹה יִיָּרֶה (yaroh yiyareh) = infinitive absolute + niphal imperfect of ירה (yarah, "to throw, shoot, cast") — "he shall surely be shot through" — with arrows or thrown projectiles. Again: execution from a distance. הַיֹּבֵל (ha-yovel) = "the ram's horn, the jubilee horn" — from יֹבֵל (yovel), which means "ram" (specifically the lead ram of the flock) and by extension the horn made from a ram. This is the same word from which "Jubilee" (the 50th year, announced by the sounding of the yovel) derives (Lev 25:10). Here the yovel is the signal instrument: when it sounds a prolonged blast, the prohibition is lifted and the people may approach. בִּמְשֹׁךְ (bimshokh) = "when [it is] drawn out, when [it] sounds a prolonged blast" — from מָשַׁךְ (mashakh, "to draw, pull, extend, prolong"). A "drawn out" blast on the horn — a single sustained note, not a series of staccato blasts. **INTERPRETIVE DIFFICULTY:** The last clause — "they shall go up on the mountain" — seems to contradict the preceding prohibition. Most interpreters understand this as: after the theophany is complete and God lifts the restriction (signaled by the yovel blast), the people may then ascend. However, vv. 21–24 reinforce the prohibition even during the theophany. LXX: οὐχ ἅψεται αὐτοῦ χείρ· ἐν γὰρ λίθοις λιθοβοληθήσεται ἢ βολίδι κατατοξευθήσεται· ἐάν τε κτῆνος ἐάν τε ἄνθρωπος, οὐ ζήσεται· ὅταν αἱ φωναὶ καὶ αἱ σάλπιγγες καὶ ἡ νεφέλη ἀπέλθῃ ἀπὸ τοῦ ὄρους, ἐκεῖνοι ἀναβήσονται ἐπὶ τὸ ὄρος ("no hand shall touch him; for with stones he shall be stoned or with a dart he shall be shot through; whether beast or man, he shall not live. When the voices and the trumpets and the cloud depart from the mountain, those ones shall go up on the mountain") — **LXX EXPANSION:** The LXX adds "the voices and the trumpets and the cloud" (αἱ φωναί, αἱ σάλπιγγες, ἡ νεφέλη) where the MT has only ha-yovel. The LXX interprets the signal as the departure of the theophanic phenomena, not the blast of a single horn. σάλπιγξ ("trumpet") for yovel; βολίς ("missile, dart") + κατατοξεύω ("shoot down with a bow") for yaroh yiyareh.

**14** And Mosheh went down [vayered] from the mountain [min-ha-har] to the people [el-ha-am], and he sanctified [vayeqaddesh] the people [et-ha-am]; and they washed [vayekhabbesu] their garments [simlatam].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֵּרֶד מֹשֶׁה מִן־הָהָר אֶל־הָעָם וַיְקַדֵּשׁ אֶת־הָעָם וַיְכַבְּסוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם (vayered Mosheh min-ha-har el-ha-am vayeqaddesh et-ha-am vayekhabbesu simlatam, "and Mosheh went down from the mountain to the people, and he sanctified the people; and they washed their garments"). **Key terms:** וַיֵּרֶד (vayered) = qal of ירד (yarad, "to go down, descend") — "and he went down." Mosheh descends; the shuttle-diplomacy pattern continues: up to God (v. 3), down to the people (v. 7), up again (v. 8b implied), down again (v. 14). וַיְקַדֵּשׁ (vayeqaddesh) = piel of קדשׁ (qadash, "to make holy, consecrate") — "and he consecrated/sanctified." Mosheh performs the sanctification rituals that YHWH commanded in v. 10. The specific rituals beyond garment-washing are not detailed here, but may have included sprinkling, anointing, or other purificatory rites. וַיְכַבְּסוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם (vayekhabbesu simlatam) = "and they washed their garments" — the people's obedient response to the command of v. 10. LXX: κατέβη δὲ Μωυσῆς ἐκ τοῦ ὄρους πρὸς τὸν λαὸν καὶ ἡγίασεν αὐτοὺς καὶ ἔπλυναν τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτῶν ("and Mosheh came down from the mountain to the people and sanctified them, and they washed their garments") — ἁγιάζω ("sanctify, make holy") for vayeqaddesh.

**15** And he said to the people [el-ha-am]: "Be prepared [heyu nekhonim] for three [li-shloshet] days [yamim]; do not approach [al-tigshu] a woman [el-ishah]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־הָעָם הֱיוּ נְכֹנִים לִשְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים אַל־תִּגְּשׁוּ אֶל־אִשָּׁה (vayomer el-ha-am heyu nekhonim li-shloshet yamim al-tigshu el-ishah, "and he said to the people: 'Be prepared for three days; do not approach a woman'"). **Key terms:** הֱיוּ נְכֹנִים (heyu nekhonim) = imperative of היה (hayah, "to be") + adjective — "be prepared/ready." The same root (kun) as v. 11 — the command to be in a state of established readiness. אַל־תִּגְּשׁוּ אֶל־אִשָּׁה (al-tigshu el-ishah) = "do not approach a woman" — from נגשׁ (nagash, "to draw near, approach") in the jussive negative (אַל + imperfect). "Approach a woman" is a euphemism for sexual intercourse (cf. Gen 20:4; Isa 8:3). The prohibition of sexual contact before a holy event is a standard ancient Near Eastern practice (cf. 1 Sam 21:4–5, where David's men must have abstained from women to eat the holy bread). The logic is not that sexuality is sinful but that seminal emission creates a state of ritual impurity (Lev 15:16–18) that is incompatible with approaching the holy. The command is addressed to the men (the grammar is masculine), which has led to feminist critique: are women addressed only through men? Are women invisible in the covenant? The text's androcentrism reflects its ancient cultural context, though the covenant itself (vv. 5–6) includes all Yisra'el. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν τῷ λαῷ· γίνεσθε ἕτοιμοι, τρεῖς ἡμέρας μὴ προσέλθητε γυναικί ("and he said to the people: 'Be ready; for three days do not approach a woman'") — προσέρχομαι ("approach, come near") for tigshu.

## The Theophany (vv. 16–25)

**16** And it was [vayehi] on the third [ba-yom ha-shelishi] day, when the morning came [bihyot ha-boqer], and there were [vayehi] thunders [qolot] and lightnings [u-veraqim] and a heavy [kaved] cloud [ve-anan] upon the mountain [al-ha-har], and the sound [ve-qol] of a shofar [exceedingly] [meod] strong [khazaq]; and all the people [kol-ha-am] who [asher] [were] in the camp [ba-makhaneh] trembled [vayekherad].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי בַיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי בִּהְיֹת הַבֹּקֶר וַיְהִי קֹלֹת וּבְרָקִים וְעָנָן כָּבֵד עַל־הָהָר וְקֹל שֹׁפָר חָזָק מְאֹד וַיֶּחֱרַד כָּל־הָעָם אֲשֶׁר בַּמַּחֲנֶה (vayehi ba-yom ha-shelishi bihyot ha-boqer vayehi qolot u-veraqim ve-anan kaved al-ha-har ve-qol shofar khazaq meod vayekherad kol-ha-am asher ba-makhaneh, "and it was on the third day, when the morning came, and there were thunders and lightnings and a heavy cloud upon the mountain, and the sound of a shofar exceedingly strong; and all the people who [were] in the camp trembled"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the sensory avalanche of theophany:** The verse piles up sensory phenomena in rapid succession: קֹלֹת (qolot) = "thunders, voices, sounds" — plural of קוֹל (qol, "voice, sound, thunder"). The same word means both "thunder" (the natural phenomenon) and "voice" (the personal communication). At Sinai, thunder IS the voice of God — or the voice of God sounds like thunder. The ambiguity is deliberate. בְּרָקִים (veraqim) = "lightnings" — plural of בָּרָק (baraq, "lightning, flash"). Lightning accompanies the voice/thunder as visual counterpart to the auditory. עָנָן כָּבֵד (anan kaved) = "a heavy/thick cloud" — כָּבֵד (kaved, "heavy, weighty, glorious"), from the same root as כָּבוֹד (kavod, "glory, weight, honor"). The cloud is "heavy" — dense, pressing, substantial, full of divine kavod. שֹׁפָר (shofar) = "ram's horn, trumpet" — the curved horn of a ram, used for signaling in war and worship. This is distinct from the yovel of v. 13. The shofar sound here has no human source — no one is blowing it. This is a supernatural shofar, a divine signal. חָזָק מְאֹד (khazaq meod) = "exceedingly strong" — from חָזָק (khazaq, "strong, mighty, firm") + מְאֹד (meod, "very, exceedingly"). The shofar blast is overwhelmingly loud. וַיֶּחֱרַד (vayekherad) = qal of חרד (kharad, "to tremble, be terrified, shake with fear") — "and he/they trembled." The root kharad denotes the physical shaking that accompanies terror — involuntary, bodily fear. LXX: ἐγένετο δὲ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ γενηθέντος πρὸς ὄρθρον καὶ ἐγίνοντο φωναὶ καὶ ἀστραπαὶ καὶ νεφέλη γνοφώδης ἐπ᾽ ὄρους Σινα, φωνὴ τῆς σάλπιγγος ἤχει μέγα· καὶ ἐπτοήθη πᾶς ὁ λαὸς ὁ ἐν τῇ παρεμβολῇ ("and it happened on the third day, as morning came, there were voices and lightnings and a dark cloud on Mount Sina, the sound of the trumpet echoed greatly; and all the people in the camp were terrified") — γνοφώδης ("dark, murky") for kaved; πτοέω ("terrify, startle") for vayekherad.

**17** And Mosheh brought out [vayotse] the people [et-ha-am] to meet [liqrat] ha-Elohim [God] from the camp [min-ha-makhaneh]; and they stationed themselves [vayityatssevu] at the base [be-takhtit] of the mountain [ha-har].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיּוֹצֵא מֹשֶׁה אֶת־הָעָם לִקְרַאת הָאֱלֹהִים מִן־הַמַּחֲנֶה וַיִּתְיַצְּבוּ בְּתַחְתִּית הָהָר (vayotse Mosheh et-ha-am liqrat ha-Elohim min-ha-makhaneh vayityatssevu be-takhtit ha-har, "and Mosheh brought out the people to meet God from the camp; and they stationed themselves at the base of the mountain"). **Key terms:** וַיּוֹצֵא (vayotse) = hiphil of יצא (yatsa, "to go out, bring out") — "and he brought out." The same causative form used for God bringing Yisra'el out of Egypt (hotsi). Now Mosheh brings them out of the camp to meet God. לִקְרַאת (liqrat) = "to meet, toward the meeting of" — from קרא (qara, "to call, meet, encounter"). The phrase liqrat ha-Elohim, "to meet God," implies mutual movement — God descends, the people advance — they come toward each other. The language of encounter, even courtship. וַיִּתְיַצְּבוּ (vayityatssevu) = hithpael of יצב (yatsav, "to station oneself, take a stand") — "and they stationed themselves, they took their stand." The reflexive-intensive form suggests a deliberate, formal positioning — like soldiers forming ranks. בְּתַחְתִּית (be-takhtit) = "at the base/bottom/lowest part of" — from תַּחַת (takhat, "beneath, under"). They stand at the foot of the mountain, looking up toward where God will appear. LXX: καὶ ἐξήγαγεν Μωυσῆς τὸν λαὸν εἰς συνάντησιν τοῦ θεοῦ ἐκ τῆς παρεμβολῆς, καὶ παρέστησαν ὑπὸ τὸ ὄρος ("and Mosheh led out the people to the meeting of God from the camp, and they stood at the foot of the mountain") — συνάντησις ("meeting, encounter") for liqrat; παρίστημι ("stand beside/before") for vayityatssevu.

**18** And Mount [ve-har] Sinai smoked [ashan] — all of it [kullo] — because [mippenei] YHWH descended [yarad] upon it in fire [ba-esh]; and its smoke [ashano] went up [vaya'al] like the smoke [ke-eshen] of a kiln [ha-kivshan], and the whole [kol] mountain [ha-har] trembled [vayekherad] exceedingly [meod].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהַר סִינַי עָשַׁן כֻּלּוֹ מִפְּנֵי אֲשֶׁר יָרַד עָלָיו יְהוָה בָּאֵשׁ וַיַּעַל עֲשָׁנוֹ כְּעֶשֶׁן הַכִּבְשָׁן וַיֶּחֱרַד כָּל־הָהָר מְאֹד (ve-har Sinai ashan kullo mippenei asher yarad alav YHWH ba-esh vaya'al ashano ke-eshen ha-kivshan vayekherad kol-ha-har meod, "and Mount Sinai smoked — all of it — because YHWH descended upon it in fire; and its smoke went up like the smoke of a kiln, and the whole mountain trembled exceedingly"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — YHWH DESCENDS IN FIRE:** This is the central event. עָשַׁן (ashan) = qal perfect of עָשַׁן (ashan, "to smoke") — "smoked." The mountain itself becomes a smoking furnace. כֻּלּוֹ (kullo) = "all of it, its entirety" — the emphatic: the entire mountain, not just the peak, is engulfed. מִפְּנֵי אֲשֶׁר (mippenei asher) = "because, on account of the fact that" — literally "from the face of [that which]." The cause is stated: YHWH descended upon it in fire. יָרַד עָלָיו יְהוָה בָּאֵשׁ (yarad alav YHWH ba-esh) = "YHWH descended upon it in fire" — yarad (qal perfect of ירד, "to descend") + alav ("upon it") + YHWH + ba-esh ("in fire, with fire"). Fire (אֵשׁ, esh) is the primary theophanic element — the burning bush (3:2), the pillar of fire (13:21), and now the entire mountain ablaze with divine presence. YHWH's mode of descent is fire. Fire both reveals (it illuminates) and destroys (it consumes); it both attracts and repels. עֲשָׁנוֹ (ashano) = "its smoke" — from עָשָׁן (ashan, "smoke") + 3ms suffix. כְּעֶשֶׁן הַכִּבְשָׁן (ke-eshen ha-kivshan) = "like the smoke of a kiln/furnace" — כִּבְשָׁן (kivshan) is a kiln or smelting furnace — an industrial structure for intense heat. The comparison to a kiln (rather than a campfire or hearth) emphasizes the overwhelming scale and intensity. The same word appears in Gen 19:28, where the smoke of Sodom's destruction goes up "like the smoke of a kivshan." וַיֶּחֱרַד כָּל־הָהָר (vayekherad kol-ha-har) = "and the whole mountain trembled" — the same verb kharad used of the people's trembling in v. 16. Now the mountain itself trembles — the inanimate rock shakes before its Maker. This is earthquake as theophanic sign. LXX: τὸ δὲ ὄρος τὸ Σινα ἐκαπνίζετο ὅλον διὰ τὸ καταβεβηκέναι ἐπ᾽ αὐτὸ τὸν θεὸν ἐν πυρί, καὶ ἀνέβαινεν ὁ καπνὸς ὡς καπνὸς καμίνου, καὶ ἐξέστη πᾶς ὁ λαὸς σφόδρα ("and Mount Sina was smoking all over because God had descended upon it in fire, and the smoke went up like the smoke of a furnace, and all the people were greatly amazed") — **LXX VARIANT:** The LXX reads πᾶς ὁ λαός ("all the people") trembling/amazed rather than the MT's כָּל־הָהָר ("the whole mountain") trembling. This is a significant difference: the MT says the mountain shook; the LXX says the people were astounded. κάμινος ("furnace, oven") for kivshan.

**19** And the sound [qol] of the shofar [was] going [holekh] and becoming exceedingly strong [ve-khazaq meod]; Mosheh would speak [yedabber] and ha-Elohim [God] would answer him [ya'annennu] with a voice [be-qol].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי קוֹל הַשֹּׁפָר הוֹלֵךְ וְחָזֵק מְאֹד מֹשֶׁה יְדַבֵּר וְהָאֱלֹהִים יַעֲנֶנּוּ בְקוֹל (vayehi qol ha-shofar holekh ve-khazaq meod Mosheh yedabber ve-ha-Elohim ya'annennu ve-qol, "and the sound of the shofar [was] going and becoming exceedingly strong; Mosheh would speak and God would answer him with a voice"). **Key terms:** הוֹלֵךְ וְחָזֵק (holekh ve-khazaq) = "going and becoming strong" — the participle holekh (from הלך, halakh, "to go, walk") + ve-khazaq ("and strong/becoming strong") is an idiom for progressive intensification: the sound keeps getting louder and louder and louder. There is no plateau, no diminution — only escalation. This is unlike any natural shofar, which fades as the player runs out of breath. The supernatural shofar builds without limit. מֹשֶׁה יְדַבֵּר (Mosheh yedabber) = "Mosheh would speak" — the imperfect (yedabber) here is iterative/habitual: Mosheh kept speaking (asking questions? presenting the people's words?). וְהָאֱלֹהִים יַעֲנֶנּוּ בְקוֹל (ve-ha-Elohim ya'annennu ve-qol) = "and God would answer him with/in a voice" — ya'annennu (qal imperfect of ענה, anah, "to answer, respond" + 3ms suffix) + be-qol ("in/with a voice/sound"). God responds audibly — with a qol, a voice that is also a sound. The same word qol that means "thunder" in v. 16 now means "voice" in God's response to Mosheh. The theophany is a conversation, not merely a spectacle. This is the dialogical nature of biblical revelation: God speaks, Mosheh speaks, God answers. LXX: καὶ ἐγίνοντο αἱ φωναὶ τῆς σάλπιγγος προβαίνουσαι ἰσχυρότεραι σφόδρα· Μωυσῆς ἐλάλει, ὁ δὲ θεὸς ἀπεκρίνατο αὐτῷ φωνῇ ("and the sounds of the trumpet were advancing, exceedingly stronger; Mosheh was speaking, and God was answering him with a voice") — προβαίνω ("advance, go forward") + ἰσχυρότερος (comparative: "stronger") for holekh ve-khazaq; ἀποκρίνομαι ("answer") for ya'annennu.

**20** And YHWH came down [vayered] upon Mount [al-har] Sinai, to the top [el-rosh] of the mountain [ha-har]; and YHWH called [vayiqra] Mosheh to the top [el-rosh] of the mountain [ha-har], and Mosheh went up [vaya'al].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֵּרֶד יְהוָה עַל־הַר סִינַי אֶל־רֹאשׁ הָהָר וַיִּקְרָא יְהוָה לְמֹשֶׁה אֶל־רֹאשׁ הָהָר וַיַּעַל מֹשֶׁה (vayered YHWH al-har Sinai el-rosh ha-har vayiqra YHWH le-Mosheh el-rosh ha-har vaya'al Mosheh, "and YHWH came down upon Mount Sinai, to the top of the mountain; and YHWH called Mosheh to the top of the mountain, and Mosheh went up"). **Key terms:** רֹאשׁ (rosh) = "head, top, summit, beginning" — from a root meaning "head." The rosh ha-har is the summit, the highest point. YHWH descends to the very top of the mountain — He does not hover in the sky but makes contact with earth. The summit becomes the meeting point of heaven and earth, the axis mundi. The repetition is emphatic: YHWH descends to the top... YHWH calls Mosheh to the top... Mosheh goes up. Three clauses, three actions, converging on a single point: the summit. The divine descent and the human ascent meet at the rosh ha-har. This is the most intimate moment in the Hebrew Bible before the mishkan is built — God and man on the same mountaintop. LXX: κατέβη δὲ κύριος ἐπὶ τὸ ὄρος τὸ Σινα ἐπὶ τὴν κορυφὴν τοῦ ὄρους· καὶ ἐκάλεσεν κύριος Μωυσῆν ἐπὶ τὴν κορυφὴν τοῦ ὄρους, καὶ ἀνέβη Μωυσῆς ("and the Lord came down upon Mount Sina, to the summit of the mountain; and the Lord called Mosheh to the summit of the mountain, and Mosheh went up") — κορυφή ("summit, peak, top of the head") for rosh.

**21** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Go down [red], warn [ha'ed] the people [ba-am] lest [pen] they break through [yehersu] to YHWH to see [lir'ot], and many [rav] of them fall [ve-nafal mimmennu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה רֵד הָעֵד בָּעָם פֶּן־יֶהֶרְסוּ אֶל־יְהוָה לִרְאוֹת וְנָפַל מִמֶּנּוּ רָב (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh red ha'ed ba-am pen-yehersu el-YHWH lir'ot ve-nafal mimmennu rav, "and YHWH said to Mosheh: 'Go down, warn the people lest they break through to YHWH to see, and many of them fall'"). **Key terms:** רֵד (red) = qal imperative of ירד (yarad, "to go down") — "go down!" The urgency is palpable: Mosheh has just arrived at the summit, and God immediately sends him back down. The theophany is so dangerous that the warning cannot wait. הָעֵד (ha'ed) = hiphil imperative of עוד (ud, "to return, repeat") or hiphil of עיד (id, "to warn, testify, bear witness") — "warn, solemnly charge, testify to." The root carries the sense of repeated, emphatic testimony — Mosheh must impress upon the people the lethal seriousness of the boundary. פֶּן (pen) = "lest, for fear that" — introduces the feared outcome. יֶהֶרְסוּ (yehersu) = qal imperfect of הרס (haras, "to break through, tear down, demolish") — "they break through." The verb haras means to demolish a wall or barrier — the people might smash through the boundary in their eagerness to see God. The desire to see (לִרְאוֹת, lir'ot, qal infinitive of ראה, ra'ah, "to see") the theophany is natural but lethal. The human impulse to gaze upon the divine without proper mediation leads to death. וְנָפַל מִמֶּנּוּ רָב (ve-nafal mimmennu rav) = "and many of them will fall" — from נפל (nafal, "to fall") — "fall" is a euphemism for dying. רָב (rav) = "many, much, great" — not just one or two but a mass casualty event. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν ὁ θεὸς πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγων· καταβὰς διαμάρτυραι τῷ λαῷ, μήποτε ἐγγίσωσιν πρὸς τὸν θεὸν κατανοῆσαι καὶ πέσωσιν ἐξ αὐτῶν πλῆθος ("and God said to Mosheh: 'Go down and solemnly warn the people, lest they draw near to God to gaze and a multitude of them fall'") — κατανοέω ("perceive, observe, gaze intently") for lir'ot; πλῆθος ("multitude") for rav.

**22** And also [ve-gam] the priests [ha-kohanim] who draw near [ha-niggashim] to YHWH shall sanctify themselves [yitqaddashu], lest [pen] YHWH break out [yifrats] against them [bahem]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְגַם הַכֹּהֲנִים הַנִּגָּשִׁים אֶל־יְהוָה יִתְקַדָּשׁוּ פֶּן־יִפְרֹץ בָּהֶם יְהוָה (ve-gam ha-kohanim ha-niggashim el-YHWH yitqaddashu pen-yifrats bahem YHWH, "and also the priests who draw near to YHWH shall sanctify themselves, lest YHWH break out against them"). **INTERPRETIVE DIFFICULTY:** Who are "the priests" (ha-kohanim) here? The Aaronic priesthood has not yet been instituted (that comes in Exodus 28–29). Several solutions have been proposed: (a) these are the firstborn, who served as priests before the Levitical system was established; (b) these are the elders or clan heads who performed sacrificial functions (cf. 24:5, "young men of the sons of Yisra'el"); (c) this is a proleptic reference — the narrator uses the later terminology; (d) there was an existing priestly class in Yisra'el from before Sinai. הַנִּגָּשִׁים (ha-niggashim) = niphal participle of נגשׁ (nagash, "to draw near, approach") — "those who draw near." The priestly function is defined by proximity to YHWH — they are "the approachers." יִתְקַדָּשׁוּ (yitqaddashu) = hithpael of קדשׁ (qadash, "to be holy") — "shall sanctify themselves." The reflexive: the priests must actively purify themselves. יִפְרֹץ (yifrats) = qal imperfect of פרץ (parats, "to break out, burst forth, breach") — "he will break out." YHWH's holiness is depicted as a force that "breaks out" (like a flood breaking through a dam) against the unprepared. The image is of divine energy that cannot be contained when proper boundaries are violated (cf. 2 Sam 6:8 — the "breach" at Perets-Uzzah when Uzzah touched the ark). LXX: καὶ οἱ ἱερεῖς οἱ ἐγγίζοντες κυρίῳ τῷ θεῷ ἁγιασθήτωσαν, μήποτε ἀπαλλάξῃ ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν κύριος ("and the priests who draw near to the Lord God, let them sanctify themselves, lest the Lord destroy [some] of them") — ἀπαλλάσσω ("release, remove, destroy") for yifrats — the LXX softens the vivid "break out" to "destroy/remove."

**23** And Mosheh said to YHWH: "The people [ha-am] are not able [lo yukhal] to go up [la'alot] to Mount [el-har] Sinai, for you yourself [attah] warned us [ha'edotah banu], saying [le'mor]: 'Set bounds [hagbel] [around] the mountain [et-ha-har] and sanctify it [ve-qiddashto].'"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־יְהוָה לֹא־יוּכַל הָעָם לַעֲלֹת אֶל־הַר סִינָי כִּי־אַתָּה הַעֵדֹתָה בָּנוּ לֵאמֹר הַגְבֵּל אֶת־הָהָר וְקִדַּשְׁתּוֹ (vayomer Mosheh el-YHWH lo-yukhal ha-am la'alot el-har Sinai ki-attah ha'edotah banu le'mor hagbel et-ha-har ve-qiddashtto, "and Mosheh said to YHWH: 'The people are not able to go up to Mount Sinai, for you yourself warned us, saying: Set bounds [around] the mountain and sanctify it'"). **Key terms:** Mosheh's response appears to push back against God's command — almost an objection: "You already told me to set boundaries; the people know they cannot approach." The tone is puzzling. Is Mosheh protesting the redundancy of the warning? Is he reassuring God that the precautions have been taken? הַעֵדֹתָה (ha'edotah) = hiphil perfect 2ms of עוד/עיד (ud/id, "to warn") + emphatic ה — "you yourself warned." The ה- ending (paragogic he) adds emphasis. בָּנוּ (banu) = "us, against us" — Mosheh includes himself among those warned. הַגְבֵּל (hagbel) = hiphil imperative of גבל (gaval, "to set a boundary") — "set a boundary!" — quoting God's earlier command from v. 12. וְקִדַּשְׁתּוֹ (ve-qiddashtto) = piel of קדשׁ (qadash) + 3ms suffix — "and sanctify it." The mountain itself must be made holy — set apart, consecrated, treated as sacred space. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν Μωυσῆς πρὸς τὸν θεόν· οὐ δυνήσεται ὁ λαὸς προσαναβῆναι πρὸς τὸ ὄρος τὸ Σινα· σὺ γὰρ διαμεμαρτύρησαι ἡμῖν λέγων· ἀφόρισαι τὸ ὄρος καὶ ἁγίασαι αὐτό ("and Mosheh said to God: 'The people will not be able to go up to Mount Sina; for you have solemnly warned us, saying: Set apart the mountain and sanctify it'") — ἀφορίζω ("set apart, separate, mark off") for hagbel.

**24** And YHWH said to him: "Go [lekh], go down [red], and you shall come up [ve-alita] — you [attah] and Aharon with you [immakh]; but the priests [ve-ha-kohanim] and the people [ve-ha-am] shall not break through [al-yehersu] to go up [la'alot] to YHWH, lest he break out [pen-yifrats] against them [bahem]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו יְהוָה לֶךְ־רֵד וְעָלִיתָ אַתָּה וְאַהֲרֹן עִמָּךְ וְהַכֹּהֲנִים וְהָעָם אַל־יֶהֶרְסוּ לַעֲלֹת אֶל־יְהוָה פֶּן־יִפְרֹץ בָּם (vayomer elav YHWH lekh-red ve-alita attah ve-Aharon immakh ve-ha-kohanim ve-ha-am al-yehersu la'alot el-YHWH pen-yifrats bam, "and YHWH said to him: 'Go, go down, and you shall come up — you and Aharon with you; but the priests and the people shall not break through to go up to YHWH, lest he break out against them'"). **Key terms:** לֶךְ־רֵד (lekh-red) = "go, go down" — two imperatives in rapid succession: לֵךְ (lekh, "go!") + רֵד (red, "go down!"). The urgency is doubled — God will not entertain Mosheh's objection. Go. Now. Down. The concession: וְעָלִיתָ אַתָּה וְאַהֲרֹן עִמָּךְ (ve-alita attah ve-Aharon immakh) = "and you shall come up — you and Aharon with you." Only Mosheh and Aharon may ascend. The hierarchy of access to the divine is firmly established: Mosheh (closest), Aharon (accompanying Mosheh), the priests (at a greater distance), and the people (furthest away, at the base). This graded approach to holiness becomes the architectural principle of the Tabernacle (most holy place -> holy place -> courtyard -> camp -> outside the camp). The repetition of the warnings from vv. 21–22 — al-yehersu ("they shall not break through"), pen-yifrats ("lest he break out") — underscores the deadly seriousness. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῷ κύριος· βάδιζε κατάβηθι καὶ ἀνάβηθι σὺ καὶ Ααρων μετὰ σοῦ· οἱ δὲ ἱερεῖς καὶ ὁ λαὸς μὴ βιαζέσθωσαν ἀναβῆναι πρὸς τὸν θεόν, μήποτε ἀπολέσῃ ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν κύριος ("and the Lord said to him: 'Go, go down, and come up — you and Aaron with you; but the priests and the people, let them not force their way up to God, lest the Lord destroy [some] of them'") — βιάζομαι ("use force, force one's way") for yehersu; ἀπόλλυμι ("destroy") for yifrats.

**25** And Mosheh went down [vayered] to the people [el-ha-am] and he said [vayomer] to them [aleihem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֵּרֶד מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הָעָם וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם (vayered Mosheh el-ha-am vayomer aleihem, "and Mosheh went down to the people and he said to them"). **INTERPRETIVE NOTE — the abrupt ending:** This final verse is strikingly incomplete. "And Mosheh went down to the people and said to them" — said what? The verse trails off without reporting the content of Mosheh's speech. The chapter ends mid-sentence, as it were, propelling the reader directly into chapter 20 and the Decalogue. The effect is literary suspension: Mosheh opens his mouth to repeat the warnings, but before we hear his words, God's voice thunders from the mountain with the Ten Words (20:1). Many scholars see this as a deliberate editorial decision: the narrator suppresses Mosheh's speech because it is immediately superseded by God's own. Others see it as evidence of redactional seaming — the transition between source documents. Either way, the abruptness is powerful: Mosheh goes down, begins to speak, and then — God speaks. LXX: κατέβη δὲ Μωυσῆς πρὸς τὸν λαὸν καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ("and Mosheh went down to the people and said to them") — the LXX also ends abruptly, preserving the MT's suspension.

## Translator's Afterword

Exodus 19 is the preparatory chapter for the most important event in Israelite religion — the giving of the Torah at Sinai. Every element in this chapter serves to heighten the gravity, terror, and privilege of what is about to happen. The covenant proposal of vv. 3–6 establishes the theological framework: YHWH has already acted in grace (v. 4, the eagle-wings rescue); now He invites a responsive relationship (v. 5, "if you will truly hear my voice"); the result will be a unique vocation among the nations (vv. 5b–6, segullah, mamlechet kohanim, goy qadosh). The theophany of vv. 16–19 deploys every sensory channel — visual (lightning, fire, smoke, cloud), auditory (thunder, shofar, voice), kinesthetic (earthquake, trembling) — to communicate that this is an event that shatters all ordinary categories. The boundary-setting (vv. 12–13, 21–24) establishes the foundational principle of graded holiness: God is approachable, but not casually; access to the divine requires preparation, mediation, and limits.

Key textual observations across the MT–LXX comparison: (1) The LXX's βασίλειον ἱεράτευμα ("royal priesthood") for mamlechet kohanim in v. 6 reshapes the political-priestly concept into a priestly-royal one, influencing NT theology (1 Pet 2:9; Rev 1:6; 5:10). (2) The LXX's addition of "and we will hear" (καὶ ἀκουσόμεθα) in v. 8 anticipates the fuller formula of 24:7. (3) The LXX's reading of "all the people" rather than "the whole mountain" in v. 18 shifts the locus of trembling from nature to humanity. (4) The LXX's "pillar of cloud" (v. 9) harmonizes with the exodus narrative where the MT preserves the distinct term "thickness of the cloud." (5) The LXX consistently softens God's "breaking out" (parats/yifrats) into "destroying" or "removing," losing the vivid hydraulic metaphor of divine holiness as an uncontainable force.
